attributes.stem: A 50-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a two-day history of a hot, swollen left big toe. He has a medical history of hypertension, diet-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus, liver cirrhosis, and gout. His regular medications include irbesartan, hydrochlorothiazide, carvedilol, lactulose and rifaximin. On examination, he appears uncomfortable. His pulse rate is 78/min, blood pressure 138/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. There is erythema, swelling and tenderness of the first metatarsophalangeal joint with limited movement of his toe. Investigations reveal a uric acid level of 0.6 mmol/L (0.20-0.45 mmol/L) and creatinine of 180 μmol/L (60–115 μmol/L).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management of the acute presentation?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with clinical features consistent with an acute gout attack. The most appropriate management in this case is prednisolone. Given the patient's comorbidities, particularly renal impairment and liver cirrhosis, prednisolone is the safest and most effective option for treating the acute gout attack. It would be important to monitor the patient's blood sugar levels whilst on steroids, although given this patient has diet-controlled diabetes, a short course of prednisolone would be unlikely to significantly derange the patient's blood sugar levels.

Colchicine is an effective treatment for acute gout, but it should be used with caution in patients with hepatic and renal impairment. In this case, it is not advisable given the patient's history of liver cirrhosis.

Indomethacin, like other NSAIDs, is contraindicated in patients with renal impairment and should be used cautiously in those with liver disease. The risk of gastrointestinal bleeding and further kidney injury makes this a less favourable option for this patient.

Allopurinol is a xanthine oxidase inhibitor used for long-term management of gout. While current guidelines do support initiating urate-lowering therapy during an acute gout attack (contrary to previous recommendations to wait until the attack resolves), it is still essential to treat the acute inflammation first. Allopurinol alone would not address the patient's acute symptoms, and the priority remains treating the inflammatory process with anti-inflammatory medications like prednisolone.

Probenecid is a uricosuric agent also used for long-term management of gout. Like allopurinol, it would not address the acute inflammation. Additionally, probenecid is contraindicated in patients with renal impairment and would be ineffective in this patient with a creatinine of 180 μmol/L.

For long-term management, this patient would benefit from urate-lowering therapy (such as allopurinol), which can be started during an acute attack (a common misconception) as this does not prolong the flare. Treatment should aim for a serum urate target below 0.36 mmol/L, or below 0.30 mmol/L in those with gouty tophi. When initiating urate-lowering therapy, prophylactic anti-inflammatory medication (low-dose colchicine or NSAIDs if not contraindicated) should be prescribed for at least 3-6 months to prevent flares that commonly occur during the initial phase of urate-lowering therapy.

While intra-articular corticosteroid injection is not presented as an option in this case, it can be an effective treatment for acute monoarticular gout. The decision between intra-articular steroid versus systemic corticosteroids is influenced by time pressure, clinician familiarity with joint injections, availability of intra-articular steroids, patient preference, and likely tolerability depending on the joint. In a primary care setting, systemic prednisolone may be more practical and accessible than joint injection, especially given that injection of the MTP joints can be quite painful for patients, and is more technically challenging to access than many of the other larger joints.
learningPoints: Prednisolone is the preferred treatment for acute gout in patients with renal impairment and liver disease when NSAIDs and colchicine are contraindicated.
Urate-lowering therapy (e.g. allopurinol) can be initiated during an acute gout attack alongside anti-inflammatory therapy for the acute flare.
Long-term management of gout requires treating to a serum urate target with appropriate prophylaxis during initiation of urate-lowering therapy.
references: McGill N. Management of acute gout. Australian Prescriber. 2004 Feb 1;27(1):10–3.

Robinson PC, Stamp LK. The management of gout: Much has changed. Aust Fam Physician. 2016 May;45(5):299-302. PMID: 27166465. Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2016/may/the-management-of-gout-much-has-changed
